title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Eight: In which Sophie leaves the castle in several directions at once (Fragment 1)
text:
Howl’s Moving Castle

Chapter Eight

I N WHICH S OPHIE LEAVES THE CASTLE IN SEVERAL DIRECTIONS AT ONCE

To Sophie’s relief, Calcifer blazed up bright and cheerful next morning. If she had not had enough of Howl, she would have been almost touched by how glad Howl was to see Calcifer. “I thought she’d done for you, you old ball of gas,” Howl said, kneeling at the hearth with his sleeves trailing in the ash.

“I was only tired,” Calcifer said. “There was some kind of drag on the castle. I’d never taken it that fast before.”

“Well, don’t let her make you do it again,” said Howl. He stood up, gracefully brushing ash off his gray-and-scarlet suit. “Make a start on that spell today, Michael. And if anyone comes from the King, I’m away on urgent private business until tomorrow. I’m going to see Lettie, but you needn’t tell him that.” He picked up his guitar and opened the door with the knob green-down, onto the wide, cloudy hills.

The scarecrow was there again. When Howl opened the door, it pitched sideways across him with its turnip face in his chest. The guitar uttered an awful twang-oing. Sophie gave a faint squawk of terror and hung on to the chair. One of the scarecrow’s stick arms was scraping stiffly round to get a purchase on the door. From the way Howl’s feet were braced, it was clear he was being shoved quite hard. There was no doubt that the thing was determined to get into the castle.

Calcifer’s blue face leaned out of the grate. Michael stood stock still beyond. “There really is a scarecrow!” they both said.

“Oh, is there? Do tell!” Howl panted. He got one foot up against the door frame and heaved. The scarecrow flew lumpishly away backward, to land with a light rustle in the heather some yards off. It sprang up instantly and came hopping toward the castle again. Howl hurriedly laid the guitar on the doorstep and jumped down to meet it. “No you don’t, my friend,” he said with one hand out. “Go back where you came from.” He walked forward slowly, still with his hand out. The scarecrow retreated a little, hopping slowly and warily backward. When Howl stopped, the scarecrow stopped too, with its one leg planted in the heather and its ragged arms tilting this way and that like a person sparring for an opening. The rags fluttering on its arms seemed a mad imitation of Howl’s sleeves.

“So you won’t go?” Howl said. And the turnip head slowly moved from side to side. No. “I’m afraid you’ll have to,” Howl said. “You scare Sophie, and there’s no knowing what she’ll do when she’s scared. Come to think of it, you scare me too.” Howl’s arms moved, heavily, as if he was lifting a large weight, until they were raised high above his head. He shouted out a strange word, which was half hidden in a crack of sudden thunder. And the scarecrow went soaring away. Up and backward it went, rags fluttering, arms wheeling in protest, up and out, and on and on, until it was a soaring speck in the sky, then a vanishing point in the clouds, and then not to be seen at all.

Howl lowered his arms and came back to the doorway, mopping his face on the back of his hand. “I take back my hard words, Sophie,” he said, panting. “That thing was alarming. It may have been dragging the castle back all yesterday. It had some of the strongest magic I’ve met. Whatever was it—all that was left of the last person you cleaned for?”

Sophie gave a weak little cackle of laughter. Her heart was behaving badly again.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Eight: In which Sophie leaves the castle in several directions at once

vocabulary.definition: Causing worry or fear.
vocabulary.example: That thing was alarming.

vocabulary.definition: Powdery residue left after the burning of a substance.
vocabulary.example: His sleeves trailing in the ash.

vocabulary.definition: Burned brightly or fiercely.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer blazed up bright and cheerful next morning.

vocabulary.definition: Prepared firmly for impact or exertion.
vocabulary.example: From the way Howl’s feet were braced, it was clear he was being shoved quite hard.

vocabulary.definition: To laugh in a loud, unpleasant way.
vocabulary.example: Sophie gave a weak little cackle of laughter.

vocabulary.definition: Noticeably happy and optimistic.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer blazed up bright and cheerful next morning.

vocabulary.definition: Showing firmness in purpose; resolute.
vocabulary.example: The thing was determined to get into the castle.

vocabulary.definition: A force that slows or resists motion.
vocabulary.example: There was some kind of drag on the castle.

vocabulary.definition: Moving lightly and quickly.
vocabulary.example: The rags fluttering on its arms seemed a mad imitation.

vocabulary.definition: In an elegant and smooth manner.
vocabulary.example: He stood up, gracefully brushing ash off his suit.

vocabulary.definition: A framework of metal bars in a fireplace, holding the burning fuel.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer’s blue face leaned out of the grate.

vocabulary.definition: The floor of a fireplace, often extending into a room and paved with brick or stone.
vocabulary.example: Howl said, kneeling at the hearth with his sleeves trailing in the ash.

vocabulary.definition: A rounded handle, as on a door.
vocabulary.example: Opened the door with the knob green-down.

vocabulary.definition: In a heavy, clumsy, or awkward way.
vocabulary.example: The scarecrow flew lumpishly away backward.

vocabulary.definition: Throw or move suddenly and with force.
vocabulary.example: The scarecrow pitched sideways across him.

vocabulary.definition: A firm hold or grip to get leverage or support.
vocabulary.example: One of the scarecrow’s stick arms was scraping stiffly round to get a purchase on the door.

vocabulary.definition: Moved back or withdrew.
vocabulary.example: The scarecrow retreated a little, hopping slowly and warily backward.

vocabulary.definition: A soft, muffled crackling sound like that caused by the movement of dry leaves or paper.
vocabulary.example: It landed with a light rustle in the heather.

vocabulary.definition: Making a harsh, grating noise by rubbing.
vocabulary.example: One of the scarecrow’s stick arms was scraping stiffly round to get a purchase on the door.

vocabulary.definition: Engaged in practice fighting or argument, mimicking combat to find an opening.
vocabulary.example: Its ragged arms tilting like a person sparring for an opening.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words used as a magical charm or incantation.
vocabulary.example: Make a start on that spell today, Michael.

vocabulary.definition: Completely motionless.
vocabulary.example: Michael stood stock still beyond.

vocabulary.definition: The most powerful supernatural energy or force.
vocabulary.example: It had some of the strongest magic I’ve met.

vocabulary.definition: Requiring immediate action or attention.
vocabulary.example: I'm away on urgent private business until tomorrow.

vocabulary.definition: Spoke or expressed in words.
vocabulary.example: The guitar uttered an awful twang-oing.
